old_claim.note: Public Museum Catalogs provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because public_art_academies is anchored to Florence in 1563, while public_museum_catalogs is now scoped to printed cabinet catalogs anchored by Imperato's 1599 publication.

source_shape.source_locator: Linda Hall Library says no museum catalogs were in print in 1580 and that Imperato published his museum book in 1599.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source places printed museum/cabinet catalogs after the 1563 public-art-academy anchor.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping the edge would make a 1599 printed catalog tradition a prerequisite for a 1563 art academy, reversing chronology and broadening the catalog node beyond its source-backed scope.

ontology_before: Public Art Academies depended directly on Public Museum Catalogs through a generic expert-inference edge.
ontology_after: Public Art Academies no longer depends on printed cabinet catalogs; the catalog node remains available for later collection and design traditions.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: public_art_academies no longer has public_museum_catalogs as a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The public_art_academies node were redated to a later academy explicitly organized around printed collection catalogs.
A source showed the 1563 Florence academy required a printed museum/cabinet catalog tradition.
The catalog node were split and an earlier art-inventory node became the prerequisite instead.
short_reason: The 1563 art-academy node should not depend on 1599 printed cabinet catalogs.
